preamble: The phonetic transformation from the diphthong /au/ to /oi/ is an attested historical sound change, primarily observed in certain Romance languages and paralleled by processes like umlaut in Germanic languages. This shift involves a complex interplay of vowel fronting and unrounding, altering the articulation of both elements of the diphthong.
phoneticEffects: This shift typically involves the fronting of the back vowel component of /au/ and often the unrounding of the rounded vowel component

examples.note: The Latin diphthong *au evolved to /oi/ in Fala, as seen in the word for 'gold'.

In the development from Old High German to Yiddish, the diphthong /ou/ (which corresponds to /au/ in Modern High German) underwent a fronting and unrounding of its second element, resulting in an /oi/-like sound.

While not a direct /au/ to /oi/ shift, German umlaut demonstrates a related process where /au/ (as in 'Baum' - tree) can correspond to /ɔʏ/ (spelled 'äu' as in 'Bäume' - trees) in inflected forms. This involves fronting and often unrounding of the second element of the diphthong, moving towards an /oi/-like pronunciation.

sources: How/why did the "oi" sound, /ɔɪ/, become so common in Yiddish? : r/linguistics - Reddit
